sources.notes: Statement and constraints: output a permutation of tree vertices that forces the mouse to finish at the trap vertex en.
Official editorial roots the tree at en and outputs vertices in decreasing depth, proving the mouse cannot remain deeper than the processed layer.

reference_candidates.notes: Luogu candidate source; not used in this review because the official editorial was sufficient.

annotation.summary: Given a start and target vertex on a tree, construct an order in which cheese appears at all vertices so the mouse ends at the target after n moves.
annotation.constraints: Total n over tests is at most 1e5; a linear DFS/BFS and output order construction is enough.
annotation.core_idea: Root the tree at the target vertex. If vertices are offered from deepest to shallowest, after all vertices at depth d or greater have been processed, the mouse cannot be deeper than d. Repeating this layer by layer forces the final position to depth 0, the target.
annotation.complexity: O(n) per test case, O(n) memory.
tricks: Root the tree at the desired final vertex.
Process vertices in decreasing distance from the target to monotonically shrink the possible final depth.

solution_variants.name: decreasing-depth-construction
solution_variants.summary: DFS/BFS from en to compute depths, bucket vertices by depth, and output buckets from largest depth down to zero.

tags.evidence: The main task is to construct a permutation that enforces the desired final behavior.
The construction depends on rooting the tree at en and using tree distances/depths.
Vertices are grouped and output by depth layers from deepest to shallowest.
A DFS from the target computes the depth buckets used by the construction.
